revision.comment: [[貝塚市]]内の[[九十九王子|熊野九十九王子社]]旧蹟
revision.text:
'''九十九王子の旧蹟'''（くじゅうくおううじのきゅうせき）では、[[九十九王子|熊野九十九王子社]]の旧蹟のうち、[[大阪府]][[貝塚市]]内に所在する旧蹟について述べる。
==九十九王子とは== ''詳細は[[九十九王子]]を参照''
九十九王子（くじゅうくおうじ）とは、[[熊野古道]]、特に[[紀伊路]]沿いに在する[[神社]]のうち、主に[[12世紀]]から[[13世紀]]にかけて、皇族・貴人の熊野詣に際して先達をつとめた熊野修験の手で急速に組織された一群の神社をいい、参詣者の守護が祈願された。
しかしながら、[[1221年]]（[[承久]]3年）の[[承久の乱]]以降、[[京都|京]]からの熊野詣が下火になり、そのルートであった紀伊路が衰退するとともに、荒廃と退転がすすんだ。[[室町時代]]以降、熊野詣がかつてのような卓越した地位を失うにつれ、この傾向はいっそう進み、[[江戸時代|近世]][[和歌山藩]]の手による顕彰も行なわれたものの、勢いをとどめるまでには至らなかった。さらに、[[明治]]以降の[[神道]]の[[国家神道]]化とそれに伴う合祀、市街化による廃絶などにより、旧社地が失われたり、比定地が不明になったものも多い。
本記事では、これら九十九王子のうち、比定地が大阪府貝塚市内に推定され、旧蹟のみとなっている王子を扱う。
==九十九王子旧蹟== 貝塚市内の九十九王子旧蹟は3社。 ===麻生川王子=== '''麻生川王子'''（あそかわおうじ）は九十九王子の13番目。[[1909年]]（[[明治42年]]）、阿里莫神社（貝塚市久保165番地）に合祀され廃絶。浅宇川王子、鹿王子との別表記あり。
半田集落の氏神として崇敬されていた神社。現在、比定地には工作機器メーカーの工場があり、その近くに全くの偶然ながら、末広大神の鎮守社が鎮座している。近隣には、[[熊野街道]]の一里塚跡があり、それを記念する道標が立てられている。 *所在地 貝塚市半田 ===鞍持王子=== '''鞍持王子'''（くらもちおうじ）は九十九王子の14番目。
『貝塚市史』『和泉誌』などに言及があり、橋本村大字王子原宮にあった旨が記述されているが、異説によれば、正福寺（貝塚市地蔵堂）の東横が社地であるともいう。
原宮の旧社は、[[1907年]]（[[明治40年]]）、南近義神社（みなみこぎじんじゃ、貝塚市王子）に合祀され廃絶。現在、比定地には鳥居と小社祠が遺されている。 *所在地 貝塚市王子 ===近木王子=== '''近木王子'''（こぎおうじ）は九十九王子の15番目。胡木新王子との別表記あり。
明治末に南近義神社に合祀され廃社となった。善正寺（貝塚市地蔵堂）の東側が旧社地に比定されるが、吉祥園寺（貝塚市王子）の南の王子村字権現にあった熊野神社（現在廃社）を旧社地とする説もある。 *所在地 貝塚市地蔵堂 ==参考文献== 西 律、1987、『熊野古道みちしるべ：熊野九十九王子現状踏査録』、荒尾成文堂（みなもと選書1）
==関連事項== *[[熊野信仰]] *[[熊野古道]] - [[紀伊路]] *[[九十九王子]] **[[九十九王子の旧蹟 (岸和田市)|池田王子]] - '''麻生川王子''' - '''近木王子''' - '''鞍持王子''' - [[九十九王子の旧蹟 (泉佐野市)|鶴原王子]] **[[九十九王子の旧蹟 (岸和田市)|岸和田市内の九十九王子旧蹟]] - [[九十九王子の旧蹟 (貝塚市)|貝塚市内の九十九王子旧蹟]] - [[九十九王子の旧蹟 (泉佐野市)|泉佐野市内の九十九王子旧蹟]]
[[Category:大阪府の神社|くしゆうくおうしのきゆうせきかいつかし]] [[Category:九十九王子|くしゆうくおうしのきゆうせきかいつかし]] [[Category:九十九王子の旧蹟|かいつかし]] [[Category:貝塚市|くしゆうくおうしのきゆうせき]]

半田集落の氏神として崇敬されていた神社。現在、比定地には工作機器メーカーの工場があり、その近くに全くの偶然ながら、末広大神の鎮守社が鎮座している。近隣には、[[熊野街道]]の一里塚跡があり、それを記念する道標が立てられている。 *所在地 貝塚市半田 ===鞍持王子=== '''鞍持王子'''（くらもちおうじ）は九十九王子の14番目。
『貝塚市史』『和泉誌』などに言及があり、橋本村大字王子原宮にあった旨が記述されているが、異説によれば、正福寺（貝塚市地蔵堂）の東横が社地であるともいう。
原宮の旧社は、[[1907年]]（[[明治40年]]）、南近義神社（みなみこぎじんじゃ、貝塚市王子）に合祀され廃絶。現在、比定地には鳥居と小社祠が遺されている。 *所在地 貝塚市王子 ===近木王子=== '''近木王子'''（こぎおうじ）は九十九王子の15番目。胡木新王子との別表記あり。
明治末に南近義神社に合祀され廃社となった。善正寺（貝塚市地蔵堂）の東側が旧社地に比定されるが、吉祥園寺（貝塚市王子）の南の王子村字権現にあった熊野神社（現在廃社）を旧社地とする説もある。 *所在地 貝塚市地蔵堂 ==参考文献== 西 律、1987、『熊野古道みちしるべ：熊野九十九王子現状踏査録』、荒尾成文堂（みなもと選書1）

'''九十九王子の旧蹟'''（くじゅうくおううじのきゅうせき）では、[[九十九王子|熊野九十九王子社]]の旧蹟のうち、[[大阪府]][[貝塚市]]内に所在する旧蹟について述べる。
== 九十九王子とは == ''詳細は[[九十九王子]]を参照''
九十九王子（くじゅうくおうじ）とは、[[熊野古道]]、特に[[紀伊路]]沿いに在する[[神社]]のうち、主に[[12世紀]]から[[13世紀]]にかけて、皇族・貴人の熊野詣に際して先達をつとめた熊野修験の手で急速に組織された一群の神社をいい、参詣者の守護が祈願された。
しかしながら、[[1221年]]（[[承久]]3年）の[[承久の乱]]以降、[[京都|京]]からの熊野詣が下火になり、そのルートであった紀伊路が衰退するとともに、荒廃と退転がすすんだ。[[室町時代]]以降、熊野詣がかつてのような卓越した地位を失うにつれ、この傾向はいっそう進み、[[江戸時代|近世]][[紀州藩]]の手による顕彰も行なわれたものの、勢いをとどめるまでには至らなかった。さらに、[[明治]]以降の[[神道]]の[[国家神道]]化とそれに伴う合祀、市街化による廃絶などにより、旧社地が失われたり、比定地が不明になったものも多い。
本記事では、これら九十九王子のうち、比定地が大阪府貝塚市内に推定され、旧蹟のみとなっている王子を扱う。

『貝塚市史』『和泉誌』などに言及があり、橋本村大字王子原宮にあった旨が記述されているが、異説によれば、正福寺（貝塚市地蔵堂）の東横が社地であるともいう。
原宮の旧社は、[[1907年]]（[[明治40年]]）、南近義神社（みなみこぎじんじゃ、貝塚市王子）に合祀され廃絶。現在、比定地には鳥居と小社祠が遺されている。 *所在地 貝塚市王子 ===近木王子=== '''近木王子'''（こぎおうじ）は九十九王子の15番目。胡木新王子との別表記あり。
明治末に南近義神社に合祀され廃社となった。善正寺（貝塚市地蔵堂）の東側が旧社地に比定されるが、吉祥園寺（貝塚市王子）の南の王子村字権現にあった熊野神社（現在廃社）を旧社地とする説もある。 *所在地 貝塚市地蔵堂 ==参考文献== 西 律、1987、『熊野古道みちしるべ：熊野九十九王子現状踏査録』、荒尾成文堂（みなもと選書1）

revision.text:
'''九十九王子の旧蹟'''（くじゅうくおううじのきゅうせき）では、[[九十九王子|熊野九十九王子社]]の旧蹟のうち、[[大阪府]][[貝塚市]]内に所在する旧蹟について述べる。
== 九十九王子とは == ''詳細は[[九十九王子]]を参照''
九十九王子（くじゅうくおうじ）とは、[[熊野古道]]、特に[[紀伊路]]沿いに在する[[神社]]のうち、主に[[12世紀]]から[[13世紀]]にかけて、皇族・貴人の熊野詣に際して先達をつとめた熊野修験の手で急速に組織された一群の神社をいい、参詣者の守護が祈願された。
しかしながら、[[承久]]3年（[[1221年]]）の[[承久の乱]]以降、[[京都|京]]からの熊野詣が下火になり、そのルートであった紀伊路が衰退するとともに、荒廃と退転がすすんだ。[[室町時代]]以降、熊野詣がかつてのような卓越した地位を失うにつれ、この傾向はいっそう進み、[[江戸時代|近世]][[紀州藩]]の手による顕彰も行なわれたものの、勢いをとどめるまでには至らなかった。さらに、[[明治]]以降の[[神道]]の[[国家神道]]化とそれに伴う合祀、市街化による廃絶などにより、旧社地が失われたり、比定地が不明になったものも多い。
本記事では、これら九十九王子のうち、比定地が大阪府貝塚市内に推定され、旧蹟のみとなっている王子を扱う。
== 九十九王子旧蹟 == 貝塚市内の九十九王子旧蹟は3社。
=== 麻生川王子 === '''麻生川王子'''（あそかわおうじ）は九十九王子の13番目。[[1909年]]（[[明治42年]]）、阿里莫神社（貝塚市久保165番地）に合祀され廃絶。浅宇川王子、鹿王子との別表記あり。
半田集落の氏神として崇敬されていた神社。現在、比定地には工作機器メーカーの工場があり、その近くに全くの偶然ながら、末広大神の鎮守社が鎮座している。近隣には、[[熊野街道]]の一里塚跡があり、それを記念する道標が立てられている。 * 所在地 貝塚市半田
=== 鞍持王子 === '''鞍持王子'''（くらもちおうじ）は九十九王子の14番目。
『貝塚市史』『和泉誌』などに言及があり、橋本村大字王子原宮にあった旨が記述されているが、異説によれば、正福寺（貝塚市地蔵堂）の東横が社地であるともいう。
原宮の旧社は、[[1907年]]（[[明治40年]]）、南近義神社（みなみこぎじんじゃ、貝塚市王子）に合祀され廃絶。現在、比定地には鳥居と小社祠が遺されている。 * 所在地 貝塚市王子
=== 近木王子 === '''近木王子'''（こぎおうじ）は九十九王子の15番目。胡木新王子との別表記あり。
明治末に南近義神社に合祀され廃社となった。善正寺（貝塚市地蔵堂）の東側が旧社地に比定されるが、吉祥園寺（貝塚市王子）の南の王子村字権現にあった熊野神社（現在廃社）を旧社地とする説もある。 * 所在地 貝塚市地蔵堂
== 参考文献 == 西 律、1987、『熊野古道みちしるべ：熊野九十九王子現状踏査録』、荒尾成文堂（みなもと選書1）
== 関連項目 == * [[熊野信仰]] * [[熊野古道]] - [[紀伊路]] * [[九十九王子]] ** （[[九十九王子の旧蹟 (岸和田市)#池田王子|池田王子]]） - '''麻生川王子''' - '''近木王子''' - '''鞍持王子''' - （[[九十九王子の旧蹟 (泉佐野市)#鶴原王子|鶴原王子]]）
{{Template:九十九王子}}
[[Category:九十九王子の旧蹟|かいつかし]] [[Category:貝塚市|くしゆうくおうしのきゆうせき]]
'''九十九王子の旧蹟'''（くじゅうくおううじのきゅうせき）では、[[九十九王子|熊野九十九王子社]]の旧蹟のうち、[[大阪府]][[岸和田市]]内に所在する旧蹟について述べる。
== 九十九王子とは == ''詳細は[[九十九王子]]を参照''
九十九王子（くじゅうくおうじ）とは、[[熊野古道]]、特に[[紀伊路]]沿いに在する[[神社]]のうち、主に[[12世紀]]から[[13世紀]]にかけて、皇族・貴人の熊野詣に際して先達をつとめた熊野修験の手で急速に組織された一群の神社をいい、参詣者の守護が祈願された。
しかしながら、[[承久]]3年（[[1221年]]）の[[承久の乱]]以降、[[京都|京]]からの熊野詣が下火になり、そのルートであった紀伊路が衰退するとともに、荒廃と退転がすすんだ。[[室町時代]]以降、熊野詣がかつてのような卓越した地位を失うにつれ、この傾向はいっそう進み、[[江戸時代|近世]][[紀州藩]]の手による顕彰も行なわれたものの、勢いをとどめるまでには至らなかった。さらに、[[明治]]以降の[[神道]]の[[国家神道]]化とそれに伴う合祀、市街化による廃絶などにより、旧社地が失われたり、比定地が不明になったものも多い。
本記事では、これら九十九王子のうち、比定地が大阪府岸和田市内に推定され、旧蹟のみとなっている王子を扱う。
== 九十九王子旧蹟 == 岸和田市内の九十九王子旧蹟は1社のみ。
=== 池田王子 === '''池田王子'''は、九十九王子の12番目。明治期の神社合祀に際して、夜疑神社（岸和田市中井町2-7-1）に合祀され廃絶した。
『和泉誌』には「今云熊野権現」、『泉州誌』には「積川王子、或云池田王子、余按、昔此辺、積川王子社領地」との記述があり、積川王子の別名から、積川神社（岸和田市積川町）との関連性が示唆されている。
[[西日本旅客鉄道|JR]][[阪和線]][[久米田駅]]に近い私有地（岸和田市下池田）内に比定される。かつては、畑の中に土が舞台上に盛り上げられた草舞台と呼ばれていた場所があり、王子跡碑があった。しかし現在では、その痕跡も失われ、畑と駐車場になっている。
* 最寄り交通機関 [[西日本旅客鉄道|JR]][[阪和線]][[久米田駅]]下車後、徒歩
== 参考文献 == 西 律、1987、『熊野古道みちしるべ：熊野九十九王子現状踏査録』、荒尾成文堂（みなもと選書1）
== 関連項目 == * [[熊野信仰]] * [[熊野古道]] - [[紀伊路]] * [[九十九王子]] ** （[[井ノ口王子]]） - '''池田王子''' - （[[九十九王子の旧蹟 (貝塚市)#麻生川王子|麻生川王子]]）
{{Template:九十九王子}}
[[Category:九十九王子の旧蹟|きしわたし]] [[Category:岸和田市|くしゆうくおうしのきゆうせき]]

九十九王子（くじゅうくおうじ）とは、[[熊野古道]]、特に[[紀伊路]]沿いに在する[[神社]]のうち、主に[[12世紀]]から[[13世紀]]にかけて、皇族・貴人の熊野詣に際して先達をつとめた熊野修験の手で急速に組織された一群の神社をいい、参詣者の守護が祈願された。
しかしながら、[[承久]]3年（[[1221年]]）の[[承久の乱]]以降、[[京都|京]]からの熊野詣が下火になり、そのルートであった紀伊路が衰退するとともに、荒廃と退転がすすんだ。[[室町時代]]以降、熊野詣がかつてのような卓越した地位を失うにつれ、この傾向はいっそう進み、[[江戸時代|近世]][[紀州藩]]の手による顕彰も行なわれたものの、勢いをとどめるまでには至らなかった。さらに、[[明治]]以降の[[神道]]の[[国家神道]]化とそれに伴う合祀、市街化による廃絶などにより、旧社地が失われたり、比定地が不明になったものも多い。
本記事では、これら九十九王子のうち、比定地が大阪府岸和田市および貝塚市内にある王子を扱う。
== 九十九王子旧蹟 == 岸和田市内の九十九王子旧蹟は1社、貝塚市内の九十九王子旧蹟は3社。
=== 池田王子 === '''池田王子'''は、[[西日本旅客鉄道|JR]][[阪和線]][[久米田駅]]に近い私有地（岸和田市下池田）内に比定される。かつては、畑の中に土が舞台上に盛り上げられた草舞台と呼ばれていた場所があり、王子跡碑があった。しかし現在では、その痕跡も失われ、畑と駐車場になっている。
『和泉誌』には「今云熊野権現」、『泉州誌』には「積川王子、或云池田王子、余按、昔此辺、積川王子社領地」との記述があり、積川王子の別名から、積川神社（岸和田市積川町）との関連性が示唆されている。明治期の神社合祀に際して、夜疑神社（岸和田市中井町2-7-1）に合祀され廃絶した。
* 最寄り交通機関 - [[西日本旅客鉄道|JR]][[阪和線]][[久米田駅]]下車後、徒歩
=== 麻生川王子 === '''麻生川王子'''（あそかわおうじ）は、[[1909年]]（[[明治42年]]）、阿里莫神社（貝塚市久保165番地）に合祀され廃絶。浅宇川王子、鹿王子との別表記あり。
半田集落の氏神として崇敬されていた神社。現在、比定地には工作機器メーカーの工場があり、その近くに全くに偶然ながら、末広大神の鎮守社が鎮座している。近隣には、[[熊野街道]]の一里塚跡があり、それを記念する道標が立てられている。 * 所在地 貝塚市半田
=== 鞍持王子 === '''鞍持王子'''（くらもちおうじ）は、[[1907年]]（[[明治40年]]）、南近義神社（みなみこぎじんじゃ、貝塚市王子）に合祀され廃絶。現在、比定地には鳥居と小社祠が遺されている。
『貝塚市史』『和泉誌』などに言及があり、橋本村大字王子原宮にあった旨が記述されているが、異説によれば、正福寺（貝塚市地蔵堂）の東横が社地であるともいう。
* 所在地 貝塚市王子
=== 近木王子 === '''近木王子'''（こぎおうじ）は明治末に南近義神社に合祀され廃社となった。胡木新王子との別表記あり。
善正寺（貝塚市地蔵堂）の東側が旧社地に比定されるが、吉祥園寺（貝塚市王子）の南の王子村字権現にあった熊野神社（現在廃社）を旧社地とする説もある。 * 所在地 貝塚市地蔵堂
== 参考文献 == 西 律、1987、『熊野古道みちしるべ：熊野九十九王子現状踏査録』、荒尾成文堂（みなもと選書1）
== 関連項目 == * [[熊野信仰]] * [[熊野古道]] - [[紀伊路]] * [[九十九王子]] ** （[[井ノ口王子]]） - '''池田王子''' - '''麻生川王子''' - '''近木王子''' - '''鞍持王子''' - （[[九十九王子の旧蹟 (泉佐野市)#鶴原王子|鶴原王子]]）
{{Template:九十九王子}}

== 関連項目 == * [[熊野信仰]] * [[熊野古道]] - [[紀伊路]] * [[九十九王子]] ** （[[井ノ口王子]]） - '''池田王子''' - '''麻生川王子''' - '''近木王子''' - '''鞍持王子''' - （[[九十九王子の旧蹟 (泉佐野市)#鶴原王子|鶴原王子]]）
{{九十九王子}}
[[Category:九十九王子の旧蹟|かいつかし]] [[Category:貝塚市|くしゆうくおうしのきゆうせき]] [[Category:岸和田市|くしゆうくおうしのきゆうせき]]

revision.comment: ページ [[九十九王子の旧蹟 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]] を [[九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]] へ移動: 改名提案にもとづく（see [[ノート:九十九王子]]）
revision.text:
{{改名提案|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|date=2009年8月|t=ノート:九十九王子}} '''九十九王子の旧蹟'''（くじゅうくおうじのきゅうせき）では、[[九十九王子|熊野九十九王子社]]の旧蹟のうち、[[大阪府]][[岸和田市]]および[[貝塚市]]内に所在する旧蹟について述べる。
== 九十九王子とは == {{Main|九十九王子}}
九十九王子（くじゅうくおうじ）とは、[[熊野古道]]、特に[[紀伊路]]沿いに在する[[神社]]のうち、主に[[12世紀]]から[[13世紀]]にかけて、皇族・貴人の熊野詣に際して先達をつとめた熊野修験の手で急速に組織された一群の神社をいい、参詣者の守護が祈願された。
しかしながら、[[承久]]3年（[[1221年]]）の[[承久の乱]]以降、[[京都|京]]からの熊野詣が下火になり、そのルートであった紀伊路が衰退するとともに、荒廃と退転がすすんだ。[[室町時代]]以降、熊野詣がかつてのような卓越した地位を失うにつれ、この傾向はいっそう進み、[[江戸時代|近世]][[紀州藩]]の手による顕彰も行なわれたものの、勢いをとどめるまでには至らなかった。さらに、[[明治]]以降の[[神道]]の[[国家神道]]化とそれに伴う合祀、市街化による廃絶などにより、旧社地が失われたり、比定地が不明になったものも多い。
本記事では、これら九十九王子のうち、比定地が大阪府岸和田市および貝塚市内にある王子を扱う。
== 九十九王子旧蹟 == 岸和田市内の九十九王子旧蹟は1社、貝塚市内の九十九王子旧蹟は3社。
=== 池田王子 === '''池田王子'''は、[[西日本旅客鉄道|JR]][[阪和線]][[久米田駅]]に近い私有地（岸和田市下池田）内に比定される。かつては、畑の中に土が舞台上に盛り上げられた草舞台と呼ばれていた場所があり、王子跡碑があった。しかし現在では、その痕跡も失われ、畑と駐車場になっている。
『和泉誌』には「今云熊野権現」、『泉州誌』には「積川王子、或云池田王子、余按、昔此辺、積川王子社領地」との記述があり、積川王子の別名から、積川神社（岸和田市積川町）との関連性が示唆されている。明治期の神社合祀に際して、夜疑神社（岸和田市中井町2-7-1）に合祀され廃絶した。
* 最寄り交通機関 - [[西日本旅客鉄道|JR]][[阪和線]][[久米田駅]]下車後、徒歩
=== 麻生川王子 === '''麻生川王子'''（あそかわおうじ）は、[[1909年]]（[[明治42年]]）、阿里莫神社（貝塚市久保165番地）に合祀され廃絶。浅宇川王子、鹿王子との別表記あり。
半田集落の氏神として崇敬されていた神社。現在、比定地には工作機器メーカーの工場があり、その近くに全くに偶然ながら、末広大神の鎮守社が鎮座している。近隣には、[[熊野街道]]の一里塚跡があり、それを記念する道標が立てられている。 * 所在地 貝塚市半田
=== 鞍持王子 === '''鞍持王子'''（くらもちおうじ）は、[[1907年]]（[[明治40年]]）、南近義神社（みなみこぎじんじゃ、貝塚市王子）に合祀され廃絶。現在、比定地には鳥居と小社祠が遺されている。
『貝塚市史』『和泉誌』などに言及があり、橋本村大字王子原宮にあった旨が記述されているが、異説によれば、正福寺（貝塚市地蔵堂）の東横が社地であるともいう。
* 所在地 貝塚市王子
=== 近木王子 === '''近木王子'''（こぎおうじ）は明治末に南近義神社に合祀され廃社となった。胡木新王子との別表記あり。
善正寺（貝塚市地蔵堂）の東側が旧社地に比定されるが、吉祥園寺（貝塚市王子）の南の王子村字権現にあった熊野神社（現在廃社）を旧社地とする説もある。 * 所在地 貝塚市地蔵堂
== 参考文献 == 西 律、1987、『熊野古道みちしるべ：熊野九十九王子現状踏査録』、荒尾成文堂（みなもと選書1）
== 関連項目 == * [[熊野信仰]] * [[熊野古道]] - [[紀伊路]] * [[九十九王子]] ** （[[井ノ口王子]]） - '''池田王子''' - '''麻生川王子''' - '''近木王子''' - '''鞍持王子''' - （[[九十九王子の旧蹟 (泉佐野市)#鶴原王子|鶴原王子]]）
{{九十九王子}}
[[Category:九十九王子の旧蹟|かいつかし]] [[Category:貝塚市|くしゆうくおうしのきゆうせき]] [[Category:岸和田市|くしゆうくおうしのきゆうせき]]

半田集落の氏神として崇敬されていた神社。現在、比定地には工作機器メーカーの工場があり、その近くに全くに偶然ながら、末広大神の鎮守社が鎮座している。近隣には、[[熊野街道]]の一里塚跡があり、それを記念する道標が立てられている。 * 所在地 貝塚市半田
=== 鞍持王子 === '''鞍持王子'''（くらもちおうじ）は、[[1907年]]（[[明治40年]]）、南近義神社（みなみこぎじんじゃ、貝塚市王子）に合祀され廃絶。現在、比定地には鳥居と小社祠が遺されている。
『貝塚市史』『和泉誌』などに言及があり、橋本村大字王子原宮にあった旨が記述されているが、異説によれば、正福寺（貝塚市地蔵堂）の東横が社地であるともいう。
* 所在地 貝塚市王子
=== 近木王子 === '''近木王子'''（こぎおうじ）は明治末に南近義神社に合祀され廃社となった。胡木新王子との別表記あり。
善正寺（貝塚市地蔵堂）の東側が旧社地に比定されるが、吉祥園寺（貝塚市王子）の南の王子村字権現にあった熊野神社（現在廃社）を旧社地とする説もある。 * 所在地 貝塚市地蔵堂
== 参考文献 == 西 律、1987、『熊野古道みちしるべ：熊野九十九王子現状踏査録』、荒尾成文堂（みなもと選書1）
== 関連項目 == * [[熊野信仰]] * [[熊野古道]] - [[紀伊路]] * [[九十九王子]] ** （[[井ノ口王子]]） - '''池田王子''' - '''麻生川王子''' - '''近木王子''' - '''鞍持王子''' - （[[九十九王子 (泉佐野市)#鶴原王子|鶴原王子]]）
{{九十九王子}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くしゆうくおうし}} [[Category:九十九王子|きしわたしかいつかし]] [[Category:大阪府の神社|くしゆうくおうしきしわたしかいつかし]] [[Category:貝塚市]] [[Category:岸和田市]]

本項目では、[[大阪府]][[岸和田市]]および[[貝塚市]]内に所在する'''[[九十九王子]]'''（くじゅうくおうじ）について述べる。
== 九十九王子とは == {{Main|九十九王子}}
九十九王子（くじゅうくおうじ）とは、[[熊野古道]]、特に[[紀伊路]]沿いに在する[[神社]]のうち、主に[[12世紀]]から[[13世紀]]にかけて、皇族・貴人の熊野詣に際して先達をつとめた熊野修験の手で急速に組織された一群の神社をいい、参詣者の守護が祈願された。
しかしながら、[[承久]]3年（[[1221年]]）の[[承久の乱]]以降、[[京都|京]]からの熊野詣が下火になり、そのルートであった紀伊路が衰退するとともに、荒廃と退転がすすんだ。[[室町時代]]以降、熊野詣がかつてのような卓越した地位を失うにつれ、この傾向はいっそう進み、[[江戸時代|近世]][[紀州藩]]の手による顕彰も行なわれたものの、勢いをとどめるまでには至らなかった。さらに、[[明治]]以降の[[神道]]の[[国家神道]]化とそれに伴う合祀、市街化による廃絶などにより、旧社地が失われたり、比定地が不明になったものも多い。
本記事では、これら九十九王子のうち、比定地が大阪府岸和田市および貝塚市内にある王子を扱う。
== 九十九王子 == 岸和田市内の九十九王子は1社、貝塚市内の九十九王子は3社。
=== 池田王子 === ''' 池田王子'''は、[[西日本旅客鉄道|JR]][[阪和線]][[久米田駅]]に近い私有地（岸和田市下池田）内に比定される。かつては、畑の中に土が舞台上に盛り上げられた草舞台と呼ばれていた場所があり、王子跡碑があった。しかし現在では、その痕跡も失われ、畑と駐車場になっている。
『和泉誌』には「今云熊野権現」、『泉州誌』には「積川王子、或云池田王子、余按、昔此辺、積川王子社領地」との記述があり、積川王子の別名から、積川神社（岸和田市積川町）との関連性が示唆されている。明治期の神社合祀に際して、夜疑神社（岸和田市中井町2-7-1）に合祀され廃絶した。
* 最寄り交通機関 - [[西日本旅客鉄道|JR]][[阪和線]][[久米田駅]]下車後、徒歩
=== 麻生川王子 === '''麻生川王子'''（あそかわおうじ）は、[[1909年]]（[[明治42年]]）、阿里莫神社（貝塚市久保165番地）に合祀され廃絶。浅宇川王子、鹿王子との別表記あり。

=== 鞍持王子 === '''鞍持王子'''（くらもちおうじ）は、[[1907年]]（[[明治40年]]）、南近義神社（みなみこぎじんじゃ、貝塚市王子）に合祀され廃絶。現在、比定地には鳥居と小社祠が遺されている。
『貝塚市史』『和泉誌』などに言及があり、橋本村大字王子原宮にあった旨が記述されているが、異説によれば、正福寺（貝塚市地蔵堂）の東横が社地であるともいう。
* 所在地 貝塚市王子
=== 近木王子 === '''近木王子'''（こぎおうじ）は明治末に南近義神社に合祀され廃社となった。胡木新王子との別表記あり。
善正寺（貝塚市地蔵堂）の東側が旧社地に比定されるが、吉祥園寺（貝塚市王子）の南の王子村字権現にあった熊野神社（現在廃社）を旧社地とする説もある。 * 所在地 貝塚市地蔵堂
== 参考文献 == 西 律、1987、『熊野古道みちしるべ：熊野九十九王子現状踏査録』、荒尾成文堂（みなもと選書1）
== 関連項目 == * [[熊野信仰]] * [[熊野古道]] - [[紀伊路]] * [[九十九王子]] ** （[[井ノ口王子]]） - '''池田王子''' - '''麻生川王子''' - '''近木王子''' - '''鞍持王子''' - （[[九十九王子 (泉佐野市)#鶴原王子|鶴原王子]]）

『和泉誌』には「今云熊野権現」、『泉州誌』には「積川王子、或云池田王子、余按、昔此辺、積川王子社領地」との記述があり、積川王子の別名から、積川神社（岸和田市積川町）との関連性が示唆されている。明治期の神社合祀に際して、夜疑神社（岸和田市中井町2-7-1）に合祀され廃絶した。
* 最寄り交通機関 - [[西日本旅客鉄道|JR]][[阪和線]][[久米田駅]]下車後、徒歩
=== 麻生川王子 === '''麻生川王子'''（あそかわおうじ）は、[[1909年]]（[[明治42年]]）、阿里莫神社（貝塚市久保165番地）に合祀され廃絶。浅宇川王子、鹿王子との別表記あり。
半田集落の氏神として崇敬されていた神社。現在、比定地には工作機器メーカーの工場があり、その近くに全くに偶然ながら、末広大神の鎮守社が鎮座している。近隣には、[[熊野街道]]の一里塚跡があり、それを記念する道標が立てられている。 * 所在地 貝塚市半田
=== 鞍持王子 === '''鞍持王子'''（くらもちおうじ）は、[[1907年]]（[[明治40年]]）、南近義神社（みなみこぎじんじゃ、貝塚市王子）に合祀され廃絶。現在、比定地には鳥居と小社祠が遺されている。
『貝塚市史』『和泉誌』などに言及があり、橋本村大字王子原宮にあった旨が記述されているが、異説によれば、正福寺（貝塚市地蔵堂）の東横が社地であるともいう。
* 所在地 貝塚市王子
=== 近木王子 === '''近木王子'''（こぎおうじ）は明治末に南近義神社に合祀され廃社となった。胡木新王子との別表記あり。
善正寺（貝塚市地蔵堂）の東側が旧社地に比定されるが、吉祥園寺（貝塚市王子）の南の王子村字権現にあった熊野神社（現在廃社）を旧社地とする説もある。 * 所在地 貝塚市地蔵堂
== 参考文献 == 西 律、1987、『熊野古道みちしるべ - 熊野九十九王子現状踏査録』、荒尾成文堂（みなもと選書1）
== 関連項目 == * [[熊野信仰]] * [[熊野古道]] - [[紀伊路]] * [[九十九王子]] ** （[[井ノ口王子]]） - '''池田王子''' - '''麻生川王子''' - '''近木王子''' - '''鞍持王子''' - （[[九十九王子 (泉佐野市)#鶴原王子|鶴原王子]]）
{{九十九王子}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くしゆうくおうし}} [[Category:九十九王子|きしわたしかいつかし]] [[Category:大阪府の神社|くしゆうくおうしきしわたしかいつかし]] [[Category:貝塚市]] [[Category:岸和田市]]

本項目では、[[大阪府]][[岸和田市]]および[[貝塚市]]内に所在する'''[[九十九王子]]'''（くじゅうくおうじ）について述べる。
== 九十九王子とは == {{Main|九十九王子}}
九十九王子（くじゅうくおうじ）とは、[[熊野古道]]、特に[[紀伊路]]沿いに在する[[神社]]のうち、主に[[12世紀]]から[[13世紀]]にかけて、皇族・貴人の熊野詣に際して先達をつとめた熊野修験の手で急速に組織された一群の神社をいい、参詣者の守護が祈願された。
しかしながら、[[承久]]3年（[[1221年]]）の[[承久の乱]]以降、[[京都|京]]からの熊野詣が下火になり、そのルートであった紀伊路が衰退するとともに、荒廃と退転がすすんだ。[[室町時代]]以降、熊野詣がかつてのような卓越した地位を失うにつれ、この傾向はいっそう進み、[[江戸時代|近世]][[紀州藩]]の手による顕彰も行なわれたものの、勢いをとどめるまでには至らなかった。さらに、[[明治]]以降の[[神道]]の[[国家神道]]化とそれに伴う合祀、市街化による廃絶などにより、旧社地が失われたり、比定地が不明になったものも多い。
本記事では、これら九十九王子のうち、比定地が大阪府岸和田市および貝塚市内にある王子を扱う。
== 九十九王子 == 岸和田市内の九十九王子は1社、貝塚市内の九十九王子は3社。

『和泉誌』には「今云熊野権現」、『泉州誌』には「積川王子、或云池田王子、余按、昔此辺、積川王子社領地」との記述があり、積川王子の別名から、積川神社（岸和田市積川町）との関連性が示唆されている。明治期の神社合祀に際して、夜疑神社（岸和田市中井町2-7-1）に合祀され廃絶した。
* 最寄り交通機関 - [[西日本旅客鉄道|JR]][[阪和線]][[久米田駅]]下車後、徒歩

=== 鞍持王子 === '''鞍持王子'''（くらもちおうじ）は、[[1907年]]（[[明治40年]]）、南近義神社（みなみこぎじんじゃ、貝塚市王子）に合祀され廃絶。現在、比定地には鳥居と小社祠が遺されている。

* 所在地 貝塚市王子
=== 近木王子 === '''近木王子'''（こぎおうじ）は明治末に南近義神社に合祀され廃社となった。胡木新王子との別表記あり。
善正寺（貝塚市地蔵堂）の東側が旧社地に比定されるが、吉祥園寺（貝塚市王子）の南の王子村字権現にあった熊野神社（現在廃社）を旧社地とする説もある。 * 所在地 貝塚市地蔵堂

revision.comment: Adding an intro to all of them. It has a magic word instead of a substitution because all of them are gonna be moved in the future to proper pagenames. See comment for more info.
revision.text:

'''{{PAGENAME}}''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview==

'''{{PAGENAME}}''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} | Image = | Location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | Coordinates = | Deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
### {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada)|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine (Kishiwada)|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
### {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
### {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|正福寺}}.
### {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka)|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} ** {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}}''' → {{ill|Tsuruhara Ōji|ja|鶴原王子}}
== References == * {{Cite book |author=Ritsu Nishi |title=Kumano Kōdō Michishirube: Kumano Kujūku Ōji Genjō Tōsaro |year=1987 |publisher=Arao Seibundō}}

'''{{PAGENAME}}''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} | Image = | Location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | Coordinates = | Deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada)|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka)|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} ** {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}}''' → {{ill|Tsuruhara Ōji|ja|鶴原王子}}

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[Ikeda Ōji]] to [[Asokawa Ōji]]
revision.text:
'''{{PAGENAME}}''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} | Image = | Location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | Coordinates = | Deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada)|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka)|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} ** {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}}''' → {{ill|Tsuruhara Ōji|ja|鶴原王子}}
== References == * {{Cite book |author=Ritsu Nishi |title=Kumano Kōdō Michishirube: Kumano Kujūku Ōji Genjō Tōsaro |year=1987 |publisher=Arao Seibundō}}

revision.text:
'''{{PAGENAME}}''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} | Image = | Location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | Coordinates = | Deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada)|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka)|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} ** {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}}''' → {{ill|Tsuruhara Ōji|ja|鶴原王子}}
== References == * {{Cite book |author=Ritsu Nishi |title=Kumano Kōdō Michishirube: Kumano Kujūku Ōji Genjō Tōsaro |year=1987 |publisher=Arao Seibundō}}

'''{{PAGENAME}}''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} | Image = | Location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | Coordinates = | Deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada)|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka)|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} ** {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}}''' → {{ill|Tsuruhara Ōji|ja|鶴原王子}}
== References == * {{Cite book |author=Ritsu Nishi |title=Kumano Kōdō Michishirube: Kumano Kujūku Ōji Genjō Tōsaro |year=1987 |publisher=Arao Seibundō}}

'''{{PAGENAME}}''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} | Image = | Location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | Coordinates = | Deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada)|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka)|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} ** {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}}''' → {{ill|Tsuruhara Ōji|ja|鶴原王子}}

'''{{PAGENAME}}''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} | Image = | Location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | Coordinates = | Deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada)|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka)|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} ** {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}}''' → {{ill|Tsuruhara Ōji|ja|鶴原王子}}

revision.text:
'''{{PAGENAME}}''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} | Image = | Location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | Coordinates = | Deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada)|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka)|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} ** {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}}''' → {{ill|Tsuruhara Ōji|ja|鶴原王子}}
== References == * {{Cite book |author=Ritsu Nishi |title=Kumano Kōdō Michishirube: Kumano Kujūku Ōji Genjō Tōsaro |year=1987 |publisher=Arao Seibundō}}

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} | Image = | Location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | Coordinates = | Deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada)|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka)|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} ** {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}}''' → {{ill|Tsuruhara Ōji|ja|鶴原王子}}

=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka)|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} ** {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}}''' → {{ill|Tsuruhara Ōji|ja|鶴原王子}}

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of Kujukuoji (Kishiwada City, Kaizuka City). <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} | Image = | Location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | Coordinates = | Deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada)|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka)|ja|熊野神社}} site.

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of Kujukuoji (Kishiwada City, Kaizuka City. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} | Image = | Location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | Coordinates = | Deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada)|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka)|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} ** {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}}''' → {{ill|Tsuruhara Ōji|ja|鶴原王子}}

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[Kujukuoji (Kishiwada City, Kaizuka City)]] to [[List of Kujūku Ōji (Kishiwada City, Kaizuka City)]]
revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of Kujukuoji (Kishiwada City, Kaizuka City. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} | Image = | Location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | Coordinates = | Deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada)|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka)|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} ** {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}}''' → {{ill|Tsuruhara Ōji|ja|鶴原王子}}
== References == * {{Cite book |author=Ritsu Nishi |title=Kumano Kōdō Michishirube: Kumano Kujūku Ōji Genjō Tōsaro |year=1987 |publisher=Arao Seibundō}}

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the regions of Kishiwada City, Kaizuka City. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} | Image = | Location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | Coordinates = | Deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada)|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka)|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} ** {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}}''' → {{ill|Tsuruhara Ōji|ja|鶴原王子}}

This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada)|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka)|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} ** {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}}''' → {{ill|Tsuruhara Ōji|ja|鶴原王子}}

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the regions of Kishiwada City, Kaizuka City.[[ja:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]] <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:new pages]][[ja:List of Kujūku Ōji (Kishiwada City, Kaizuka City)]] ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} | Image = | Location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | Coordinates = | Deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada)|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka)|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} ** {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}}''' → {{ill|Tsuruhara Ōji|ja|鶴原王子}}

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the regions of Kishiwada City, Kaizuka City.[[ja:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]] <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:new pages]][[ja:九十九王子 (Kishiwada City, Kaizuka City)]] ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} | Image = | Location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | Coordinates = | Deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada)|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka)|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} ** {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}}''' → {{ill|Tsuruhara Ōji|ja|鶴原王子}}

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the regions of Kishiwada City, Kaizuka City.[[ja:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]] <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:new pages]][[ja:九十九王子 (Kishiwada City, Kaizuka City)]] ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} | Image = | Location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | Coordinates = | Deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada)|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka)|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} ** {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}}''' → '''{{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}}''' → {{ill|Tsuruhara Ōji|ja|鶴原王子}}
== References == * {{Cite book |author=Ritsu Nishi |title=Kumano Kōdō Michishirube: Kumano Kujūku Ōji Genjō Tōsaro |year=1987 |publisher=Arao Seibundō}}
