revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the regions of Kaizuka.[[ja:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (Kaizuka)]] ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} | image = | location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | coordinates = | deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada)|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka)|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} ''' {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社|fr|Yagi-jinja|lt=Ikeda Ōji|error=List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Asokawa Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kogi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kuramochi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Izumisano City)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Tsuruhara Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the regions of Kaizuka.[[ja:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]] <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (Kaizuka)]] ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} | image = | location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | coordinates = | deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada)|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka)|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka)|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} ''' {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社|fr|Yagi-jinja|lt=Ikeda Ōji|error=List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Asokawa Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kogi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kuramochi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Izumisano City)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Tsuruhara Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the regions of Kaizuka.[[ja:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]] <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kaizuka)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (Kaizuka)]] ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} | image = | location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | coordinates = | deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} ''' {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社|fr|Yagi-jinja|lt=Ikeda Ōji|error=Kaizuka)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Asokawa Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kogi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kuramochi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Izumisano City)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Tsuruhara Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}'''
== References == * {{Cite book |author=Ritsu Nishi |title=Kumano Kōdō Michishirube: Kumano Kujūku Ōji Genjō Tōsaro |year=1987 |publisher=Arao Seibundō}}[[ja:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kaizuka)-qq]] [[en:Kaizuka)]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kaizuka)-qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kaizuka, Osaka]] [[Category:History of Kishiwada, Osaka]] [[Category:神社関連テンプレート|くしゆくおうし]] [[Category:九十九王子|-てんふれえと]] [[ja:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]][[zh:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kaizuka)-qq]] [[en:Kaizuka)]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kaizuka)-qq]] {{citation needed|bottom}}

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the regions of Kaizuka.[[ja:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]] <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kaizuka)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (Kaizuka)]] ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} | image = | location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | coordinates = | deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} ''' {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社|fr|Yagi-jinja|lt=Ikeda Ōji|error=Kaizuka)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Asokawa Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kogi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kuramochi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Izumisano City)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Tsuruhara Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}'''

This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} ''' {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社|fr|Yagi-jinja|lt=Ikeda Ōji|error=Kaizuka)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Asokawa Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kogi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kuramochi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Izumisano City)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Tsuruhara Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}'''

These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.

The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the regions of Kaizuka.[[ja:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]] <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kaizuka)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (Kaizuka)]] ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} | image = | location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | coordinates = | deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} ''' {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社|fr|Yagi-jinja|lt=Ikeda Ōji|error=Kaizuka)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Asokawa Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kogi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kuramochi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Izumisano City)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Tsuruhara Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}'''

== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} ''' {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社|fr|Yagi-jinja|lt=Ikeda Ōji|error=Kaizuka)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Asokawa Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kogi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kuramochi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Izumisano City)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Tsuruhara Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}'''

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the regions of Kaizuka.[[ja:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]] <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
[[Category:qq deprecated open]]<!--Kaizuka)-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[ja:九十九王子 (Kaizuka)]] ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} | image = | location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | coordinates = | deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} ''' {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社|fr|Yagi-jinja|lt=Ikeda Ōji|error=Kaizuka)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Asokawa Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kogi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kuramochi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Izumisano City)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Tsuruhara Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}'''

This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} ''' {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社|fr|Yagi-jinja|lt=Ikeda Ōji|error=Kaizuka)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Asokawa Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kogi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kuramochi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Izumisano City)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Tsuruhara Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}'''

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the regions of Kaizuka.[[ja:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]] <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--Kaizuka)--> [[ja:九十九王子 (Kaizuka)]] ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} | image = | location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | coordinates = | deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} ''' {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社|fr|Yagi-jinja|lt=Ikeda Ōji|error=Kaizuka)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Asokawa Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kogi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kuramochi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Izumisano City)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Tsuruhara Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}'''
== References == * {{Cite book |author=Ritsu Nishi |title=Kumano Kōdō Michishirube: Kumano Kujūku Ōji Genjō Tōsaro |year=1987 |publisher=Arao Seibundō}}[[ja:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category:tier 1]] <!--Kaizuka)--> [[en:Kaizuka)]] <!--Kaizuka)--> [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kaizuka, Osaka]] [[Category:History of Kishiwada, Osaka]] [[Category:神社関連テンプレート|くしゆくおうし]] [[Category:九十九王子|-てんふれえと]] [[ja:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]][[zh:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category:tier 1]] <!--Kaizuka)--> [[en:Kaizuka)]] <!--Kaizuka)--> {{citation needed|bottom}}

Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka|ja|熊野神社}} site.

<!--Kaizuka)--> [[ja:九十九王子 (Kaizuka)]] ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} | image = | location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | coordinates = | deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} ''' {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社|fr|Yagi-jinja|lt=Ikeda Ōji|error=Kaizuka)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Asokawa Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kogi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kuramochi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Izumisano City)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Tsuruhara Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}'''

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the regions of Kaizuka.[[ja:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]] <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--Kaizuka)--> [[ja:九十九王子 (Kaizuka)]] ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} | image = | location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | coordinates = | deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} ''' {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社|fr|Yagi-jinja|lt=Ikeda Ōji|error=Kaizuka)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Asokawa Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kogi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kuramochi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Izumisano City)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Tsuruhara Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}'''
== References == * {{Cite book |author=Ritsu Nishi |title=Kumano Kōdō Michishirube: Kumano Kujūku Ōji Genjō Tōsaro |year=1987 |publisher=Arao Seibundō}}[[ja:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]]<!--Kaizuka)--> [[en:Kaizuka)]] <!--Kaizuka)--> [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kaizuka, Osaka]] [[Category:History of Kishiwada, Osaka]] [[Category:神社関連テンプレート|くしゆくおうし]] [[Category:九十九王子|-てんふれえと]] [[ja:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]][[zh:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]]<!--Kaizuka)--> [[en:Kaizuka)]] <!--Kaizuka)--> {{citation needed|bottom}}

This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} ''' {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社|fr|Yagi-jinja|lt=Ikeda Ōji|error=Kaizuka)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Asokawa Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kogi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kuramochi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Izumisano City)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Tsuruhara Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}'''

This article covers the shrines in the regions of Kaizuka.[[ja:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]] <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--Kaizuka)--> [[ja:九十九王子 (Kaizuka)]] ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} | image = | location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | coordinates = | deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} ''' {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社|fr|Yagi-jinja|lt=Ikeda Ōji|error=Kaizuka)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Asokawa Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kogi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kuramochi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Izumisano City)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Tsuruhara Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}'''

This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} ''' {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社|fr|Yagi-jinja|lt=Ikeda Ōji|error=Kaizuka)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Asokawa Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kogi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kuramochi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Izumisano City)|ja|九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Tsuruhara Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}'''
== References == * {{Cite book |author=Ritsu Nishi |title=Kumano Kōdō Michishirube: Kumano Kujūku Ōji Genjō Tōsaro |year=1987 |publisher=Arao Seibundō}}[[ja:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]]<!--Kaizuka)--> [[en:Kaizuka)]] <!--Kaizuka)--> [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kaizuka, Osaka]] [[Category:History of Kishiwada, Osaka]] [[Category:神社関連テンプレート|くしゆくおうし]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji|-てんふれえと]] [[ja:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]][[zh:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]]<!--Kaizuka)--> [[en:Kaizuka)]] <!--Kaizuka)--> {{citation needed|bottom}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|九十九王子" to "|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji"
revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the regions of Kaizuka.[[ja:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]] <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--Kaizuka)--> [[ja:九十九王子 (Kaizuka)]] ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} | image = | location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | coordinates = | deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} ''' {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社|fr|Yagi-jinja|lt=Ikeda Ōji|error=Kaizuka)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Asokawa Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kogi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kuramochi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Izumisano City)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Tsuruhara Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}'''

This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka|ja|熊野神社}} site.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} ''' {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社|fr|Yagi-jinja|lt=Ikeda Ōji|error=Kaizuka)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Asokawa Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kogi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kuramochi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Izumisano City)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Tsuruhara Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}'''
== References == * {{Cite book |author=Ritsu Nishi |title=Kumano Kōdō Michishirube: Kumano Kujūku Ōji Genjō Tōsaro |year=1987 |publisher=Arao Seibundō}}[[ja:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)]][[Category:qq]] <!--Kaizuka)--> [[en:Kaizuka)]] <!--Kaizuka)--> [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kaizuka, Osaka]] [[Category:History of Kishiwada, Osaka]] [[Category:神社関連テンプレート|くしゆくおうし]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji|-てんふれえと]] [[ja:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]][[zh:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)]][[Category:qq]] <!--Kaizuka)--> [[en:Kaizuka)]] <!--Kaizuka)--> {{citation needed|bottom}}

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the regions of Kaizuka.[[ja:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]] <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--Kaizuka)--> [[ja:九十九王子 (Kaizuka)]] ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} | image = | location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | coordinates = | deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka|ja|熊野神社}} site. == See Also! == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} ''' {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社|fr|Yagi-jinja|lt=Ikeda Ōji|error=Kaizuka)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Asokawa Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kogi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Kuramochi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Izumisano City)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji (岸和田市・貝塚市)|en|lt=Tsuruhara Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}'''

This article covers the shrines in the regions of Kaizuka.[[ja:九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)]] <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--Kaizuka)--> [[ja:九十九王子 (Kaizuka)]] ==Overview== {{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} | image = | location = {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, Japan | coordinates = | deity = | Affiliation = {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野信仰}} | Established = | Map = }}
This article covers the '''{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}''' shrines located within {{ill|Kishiwada, Osaka|ja|岸和田市}} and {{ill|Kaizuka, Osaka|ja|貝塚市}}, which were part of the historic pilgrimage route to {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}.
== Overview of Kujūku Ōji == {{Main|{{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}}}}
The Kujūku Ōji shrines refer to a group of sacred sites along the {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, especially the {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} route, established during the 12th and 13th centuries by {{ill|Kumano Shugendō|ja|熊野修験}} practitioners. These shrines served to protect pilgrims, including members of the imperial family and nobility, on their journeys to the sacred sites of Kumano.
Following the decline of the {{ill|Kumano Pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}} after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, many of these shrines fell into disrepair. Despite some restoration efforts during the Edo period by the {{ill|Kishū Domain|ja|紀州藩}}, many shrines were lost due to urbanization and the State Shinto reforms of the Meiji era.
This article focuses on the Kujūku Ōji shrines identified within Kishiwada and Kaizuka.
== Kujūku Ōji Shrines == There is one known Ōji shrine in Kishiwada and three in Kaizuka.
=== {{ill|Ikeda Ōji|ja|池田王子}} === Located near {{ill|Kumeda Station|ja|久米田駅}} on the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}}, the former site of Ikeda Ōji is now private land in Shimo-Ikeda, Kishiwada. The shrine was believed to be associated with {{ill|Sekigawa Shrine (Kishiwada|ja|積川神社}} and was merged into {{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社}} during the Meiji period.
=== {{ill|Asokawa Ōji|ja|麻生川王子}} === Also known as Aso River Ōji, it was merged into {{ill|Arimaku Shrine|ja|阿里莫神社}} in Kaizuka in 1909. The shrine was venerated by the Handa community, and although its original location is now occupied by a factory, nearby landmarks commemorate its historical significance.
=== {{ill|Kuramochi Ōji|ja|鞍持王子}} === Merged into {{ill|Minami Kogi Shrine|ja|南近義神社}} in Kaizuka in 1907, the former shrine site still has a small torii gate and shrine structure. Some historical records suggest alternative locations, including near {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kaizuka|ja|正福寺}}.
=== {{ill|Kogi Ōji|ja|近木王子}} === Also known as Kogi Shin Ōji, it was merged into Minami Kogi Shrine in the late Meiji era. The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka|ja|熊野神社}} site. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} ''' {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社|fr|Yagi-jinja|lt=Ikeda Ōji|error=Kaizuka)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|en|lt=Asokawa Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|en|lt=Kogi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|en|lt=Kuramochi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Izumisano City)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|en|lt=Tsuruhara Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}'''

The former shrine site is debated, with possible locations near {{ill|Zenshō-ji (Kaizuka|ja|善正寺}} or the former {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kaizuka|ja|熊野神社}} site. == See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Kōdō|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kiiji|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} ''' {{ill|Inokuchi Ōji|ja|井ノ口王子}} → '''{{ill|Yagi Shrine|ja|夜疑神社|fr|Yagi-jinja|lt=Ikeda Ōji|error=Kaizuka)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|en|lt=Asokawa Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|en|lt=Kogi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → '''{{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|en|lt=Kuramochi Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}''' → {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Izumisano City)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=List of Kujūku Ōji (Kaizuka)|en|lt=Tsuruhara Ōji|error=九十九王子 (岸和田市・貝塚市)|oji=王子}}'''
